content_en:
The mu'amalat section of Al-Fiqh al-Islami wa-Adillatuhu covers Islamic commercial and financial law in exhaustive detail, making it an essential reference for contemporary Islamic finance scholarship. Dr. al-Zuhayli addresses the full range of financial transactions recognized by classical fiqh, with particular attention to the differences between schools and their implications for modern commercial practice.

The law of sale (bay') is treated comprehensively. The essential elements of a valid contract — the two contracting parties, the offer and acceptance, and the subject matter — are explained with the conditions each school imposes. The Hanafi concept of khiyar al-majlis (option of the session), rejected by the Hanafi school but affirmed by the Shafi'i and Hanbali schools based on hadith evidence, is one example of the type of scholarly difference the encyclopedia clarifies.

Riba is treated as one of the most important topics in Islamic financial law. Dr. al-Zuhayli traces the prohibition from its Quranic roots through the hadith evidence specifying the six ribawi goods, and then addresses the question of how the prohibition extends to contemporary financial instruments. He presents the scholarly positions on bank interest: the overwhelming consensus that it constitutes riba and is therefore prohibited, and the minority dissenting voices that argued for its permissibility under certain conditions, evaluating each with reference to the classical evidence.

The book covers sukuk (Islamic bonds), murabaha (cost-plus financing), ijarah wa-iqtina (lease-to-own), and other contemporary Islamic finance instruments, examining them against classical principles. Dr. al-Zuhayli's treatment of shirkah (partnership), mudarabah (profit-sharing investment), and musharakah (equity partnership) provides the classical jurisprudential basis that contemporary Islamic banking institutions draw upon. The chapter on takaful (Islamic insurance) versus conventional insurance offers one of the clearest scholarly analyses of why conventional insurance is problematic from a Shari'ah perspective and what makes takaful a permissible alternative. For practitioners in Islamic finance, Al-Fiqh al-Islami wa-Adillatuhu functions as an indispensable desk reference that grounds contemporary financial structuring discussions in the classical jurisprudential reasoning from which modern Islamic finance derives its legitimacy. The work's simultaneous command of all four schools enables the kind of comparative analysis that Islamic finance scholars need when evaluating whether a particular instrument meets the Shari'ah requirements recognized across the tradition.
